search_results.title: Distributed representations of atoms and materials for machine learning
search_results.snippet: To build effective machine learning models of material chemistry, useful machine-based representations of atoms and their compounds are required, with distributed representations derived from chemical formulas through pooling of atom representations.

search_results.title: Towards Analogy-Based Explanations in Machine Learning
search_results.snippet: Analogy is particularly challenging for machines because it requires relational structures to be represented such that they can be flexibly applied across diverse domains of experience. Applying analogical structure from one atom-like representation to another is a hard problem.

search_results.title: Learning to Make Analogies by Contrasting Abstract Relational Structure
search_results.snippet: The critical factor for inducing analogical reasoning capacity is careful attention to the choice of data and the manner in which it is presented to the model.

search_results.title: The Unpredictable Abilities Emerging From Large AI Models | Quanta Magazine
search_results.snippet: Models still struggle to make accurate predictions about the future, and Large language models often produce outputs with high confidence even when incorrect, due to miscalibrated uncertainty. The hard things here are exactly where the model is uncertain.

search_results.title: Solomonoff-Inspired Hypothesis Ranking with LLMs for Prediction Under Uncertainty
search_results.snippet: Hard prediction problems require models to maintain uncertainty over hypotheses. Solomonoff induction provides a theoretical framework for ranking hypotheses under uncertainty.

search_results.title: Future Is Unevenly Distributed: Forecasting Ability of LLMs Depends on What We're Asking
search_results.snippet: LLM forecasting ability is heterogeneous across topics. The hard things are predictions that lack base rate signal and where the model is uncertain about which features matter.

search_results.title: Structural Correspondence | Representation in Cognitive Science | Oxford Academic
search_results.snippet: A large body of work examines the idea that structural correspondence or isomorphism should be an ingredient in a theory of content, with a central problem being to specify which kinds of relation can enter into the correspondence.

search_results.title: Towards a category theory approach to analogy: Analyzing re-representation and acquisition of numerical knowledge - PMC
search_results.snippet: The key structural constraint underlying analogical mapping and inference is a pressure to establish an isomorphism between the elements of the source and target. Quasi-homomorphism preserves the structure with exceptions where the map would not preserve the structure.

search_results.title: Correspondence-based analogies for choosing problem representations | IEEE Xplore
search_results.snippet: Correspondence-based analogies for choosing problem representations: the structural correspondence approach addresses the analogy mapping problem in open computational settings.

sub_claims.text: the analogy is not merely a surface metaphor

search_results.title: Analogy - Wikipedia
search_results.snippet: Two things are analogous not because they look the same on the surface, but because they share a pattern of relationships. Analogy goes deeper than mere surface similarity.

search_results.title: Metaphor, examples and analogy: the craft of explanation
search_results.snippet: Metaphor is not merely a bit of rhetorical polish. Rather, metaphor and analogy are not ornaments pinned onto thought after the fact. They are part of how thinking works.

search_results.title: Analogy vs. Metaphor: What's the Difference?
search_results.snippet: The point of an analogy is not merely to show, but also to explain. In contrast, where we use analogies to explain, we apply metaphors to make readers feel.
